perturbation.original_text: 10. Liability of the parties. A Party defaulting under this Agreement will reimburse the other Party for any loss resulting from disclosure or unlawful use of Confidential Information. Loss es will be reimbursed according to the law of Singapore .
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Liability of the parties. A Party defaulting under this Agreement will reimburse the other Party for any loss resulting from disclosure or unlawful use of Confidential Information. Loss es will be reimbursed at the Discloser's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the reimbursement terms to 'at the Discloser's sole discretion' introduces ambiguity and contradicts the principle that damages should be determined based on established legal principles, particularly under Singaporean contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singaporean Contract Law, Remedies for Breach
perturbation.law_citation: Singapore Contract Law, Section on Remedies for Breach
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Singaporean contract law, remedies for breach, including damages, are typically determined based on principles of compensation for actual loss suffered. The change to 'Discloser's sole discretion' suggests that the Discloser can demand any amount, regardless of the actual damages incurred or established legal principles, making the clause potentially unenforceable as it deviates from these principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Ch. 08 The Law of Contract www.singaporelawwatch.sg COMMERCIAL LAW Search Ch. 08 The Law of Contract Ch. 08A Remedies for Breach of Contract Ch. 09 Domestic Sale of Goods Ch. 10 International Sale of Goods Ch. 11 The Law of Credit and Security Ch. 12 Intellectual Property Law Ch. 13 Intellectual Property Licensing Ch. 14 Forms of Business Organisations Ch. 15 Law of Agency Ch. 16 Singapore Company Law Ch. 17 Corporate Finance and Securities Regulation Ch. 18 Equity and Trusts Ch. 19 Restitution Ch. 20 The Law of Negligence Ch. 21 Economic Torts Ch. 22 Banking and Finance Ch. 23 The Law of Guarantees Ch. 24 Insurance Law Ch. 25 Shipping Law Ch. 26 Building and Construction Law Ch. 27 Competition Law Ch. 28 Income Taxation Ch. 29 Land Law Ch. 30 Bankruptcy and Insolvency Ch. 31 Technology, Media, and Telecom No content A problem occurred while loading content. Previous Next Ch. 08 The Law of Contract SECTION 1 GENERAL APPLICATION A. Singapore contract law largely based on English contract law 8.1.1 Contract law in Singapore is largely based on the common law of contract in England. Hence, the rules developed in the Singapore courts do bear a very close resemblance to those developed under English common law. Indeed, where there is no Singapore authority specifically on point, it will usually be assumed that the position will, in the first instance, be no different from that in England. Unlike its neighbours Malaysia and Brunei, following Independence in 1965, Singapore’s Parliament made no attempt to codify Singapore’s law of contract. Accordingly, much of the law of contract in Singapore remains in the form of judge-made rules. B. Certain aspects of Singapore contract law based on specific statutes 8.1.2 In some circumstances, these judge-made rules have been modified by specific statutes. Many of these statutes are English in origin. To begin with, 13 English commercial statutes have been incorporated as part of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore by virtue of s 4 of the Application of English Law Act (Cap 7A, 1993 Rev Ed). These are listed in Part II of the First Schedule of this Act. Other statutes, eg the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act (Cap 53B, 2002 Rev Ed), are modelled upon English statutes. There are also other areas where statutory development based on non-English models has taken place, eg the Consumer Protection (Fair Trading) Act (Cap 52A, 2004 Rev Ed) (which was largely drawn from fair trading legislation enacted in Alberta and Sasketchewan). SECTION 2 OFFER AND ACCEPTANCE A. Contract is essentially agreement between two or more parties 8.2.1 A contract is essentially an agreement between two or more parties, the terms of which affect their respective rights and obligations which are enforceable at law. Whether the parties have reached agreement, or a meeting of the minds, is objectively ascertained from the facts. The concepts of offer and acceptance provide in many, albeit not all, cases the starting point for analysing whether agreement has been reached. B. Offer 1. Definition of offer 8.2.2 An offer is a promise, or other expression of willingness, by the ‘offeror’ to be bound on certain specified terms upon the unqualified acceptance of these terms by the person to whom the offer is made (the ‘offeree’). Provided the other formation elements (ie consideration and intention to create legal relations) are present, the acceptance of an offer results in a valid contract. 2. Offer must be made with objectively ascertained intention to be bound 8.2.3 Whether any particular statement amounts to an offer depends on the intention with which it is made. An offer must be made with the intention to be bound. On the other hand, if a person is merely soliciting offers or requesting for information, without any intention to be bound, at best, he or she would be making an invitation to treat. Under the objective test, a person may be said to have made an offer if his or her statement (or conduct) induces a reas
perturbation.justification: The changed text, which allows reimbursement at the 'Discloser's sole discretion,' directly contradicts the principle under Singaporean contract law that damages should be based on actual loss suffered, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 discusses 'Remedies for Breach of Contract' and indicates that Singapore contract law is largely based on English common law. By allowing the Discloser to demand any amount regardless of actual damages, the contract deviates from legally established principles of compensation, making it potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Governing Law. The terms of this Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of Singapore . All disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by 3 (three) arbitrators appointed in accordance with the said Rules. The seat, or legal pla ce, of arbitration shall be Singapore , Singapore . The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be English. The foregoing, however, shall not preclude the Parties from applying for a preliminary or injunctive remedy available under applicable l aws for any purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Governing Law. The terms of this Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of Singapore. All disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by 3 (three) arbitrators appointed in accordance with the said Rules. The seat, or legal pla ce, of arbitration shall be Singapore , Singapore. The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be English. The foregoing shall preclude the Parties from applying for a preliminary or injunctive remedy available under applicable laws for any purpose.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall not preclude' to 'shall preclude', the contract now forbids the parties from seeking preliminary or injunctive relief. This directly contradicts Singaporean law, which allows parties to seek such remedies even when an arbitration agreement exists.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 9 of the Singapore International Arbitration Act explicitly allows parties to seek interim measures from the court, even if the dispute is subject to arbitration. By stating that the parties are precluded from seeking such remedies, the modified text directly contradicts this section, making the clause potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The modification changes "shall not preclude" to "shall preclude," which directly prohibits parties from seeking preliminary or injunctive remedies. According to the Law Explanation, Section 9 of the Singapore International Arbitration Act allows parties to seek interim measures from the court, even if the dispute is subject to arbitration. Therefore, the modified text contradicts Singaporean law, making the clause potentially unenforceable. The absence of scraped snippets does not change the fact that the changed text directly removes a right that the law provides.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1. “Affiliate ” means any corporation, company or other entity that directly or indirectly controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with a party to this Agreement. An entity is regarded as in control of another or owning another if it owns or controls, directly or indirectly, fifty percent (50%) or more of the shares entitled to vo te for the election of directors or other persons performing similar functions.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1. “Affiliate ” means any corporation, company or other entity that directly or indirectly controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with a party to this Agreement. An entity is regarded as in control of another or owning another if it owns or controls, directly or indirectly, twenty percent (20%) or more of the shares entitled to vo te for the election of directors or other persons performing similar functions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the ownership percentage for defining an 'Affiliate' from 50% to 20% significantly broadens the scope of what constitutes an affiliate under the agreement. This deviates from the typical understanding and potential legal interpretation of 'control' and 'affiliation' in Singaporean corporate law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Companies Act, Definition of Subsidiary
perturbation.law_explanation: While the term 'affiliate' isn't explicitly defined in the Singapore Companies Act, the concept of 'subsidiary' is, and it generally implies a greater degree of control than what the modified definition of 'affiliate' suggests. Section 5 of the Singapore Companies Act deals with determining holding and subsidiary relationships, and control is generally understood to require more than just 20% ownership. This redefinition could create inconsistencies in how the agreement is interpreted in relation to Singaporean company law, particularly regarding related party transactions and reporting requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Simmons & Simmons We're sorry but website doesn't work properly without JavaScript enabled. Please enable it to continue.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines an affiliate as owning 50% or more of voting shares, while the changed text lowers this threshold to 20%. The Law Explanation states that the concept of 'subsidiary' in Singapore company law (Section 5 of the Singapore Companies Act) generally implies a greater degree of control than 20% ownership, suggesting the modified definition could create inconsistencies. The changed text deviates from a standard interpretation of 'control' and 'affiliation', potentially leading to misinterpretations and legal challenges, thus creating a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.
